body: Inspect whether the page uses consistent terminology that matches the vocabulary of the target audience — avoiding internal jargon, technical codes, or branded terms that users are unlikely to recognize — and whether the same concept is labeled identically across all touchpoints on the page and across the site. Positive signals include labels and button copy that use words familiar to the target audience, consistent naming of the same action or entity across every instance on the page (e.g., 'Add to Cart' never alternating with 'Add to Bag' on the same site), and interface copy at a reading level appropriate to the audience without oversimplifying for expert users. Negative signals include organization-internal terms or product-team jargon used in user-facing labels, the same feature or entity described with different names in different places on the same page or across pages, and technical vocabulary presented without inline definition for non-expert audiences. When violations are found, recommend conducting a terminology audit across all page templates, standardizing label copy in the design system, and replacing internal-team nomenclature with terms validated as familiar to the target user segment. Nielsen Norman Group's plain language guidance states: 'Pick the term that is most familiar to your audience — the word more likely to be used by them,' and emphasizes that 'Remove slang, idioms, and branded terms' since organization-specific terminology takes considerable time to enter common usage.

benchmark.standard_text: Same concept labeled identically across all instances; no internal jargon in user-facing copy; terminology matches vocabulary familiar to the target audience

provenance.citation_text: From Nielsen Norman Group's guidance on plain language: "Pick the term that is most familiar to your audience — the word more likely to be used by them." The piece emphasizes that familiar words are comprehended faster than obscure alternatives, as they activate more readily in memory. On avoiding jargon: "Remove slang, idioms, and branded terms." The author notes that organization-specific terminology takes considerable time to enter common usage and should be replaced or explicitly defined. Additionally, complex language creates a pretentious tone that risks alienating readers. On technical terms: when specialized vocabulary is necessary, "explain them first." However, the guidance recognizes that jargon can serve expert audiences effectively when shared within professional groups. For example, "ventricular tachycardia" functions as efficient shorthand for physicians who understand the concept. The core recommendation is matching word complexity to your specific audience's knowledge level. This prevents both confusion and condescension — oversimplifying alienates experts, while using unfamiliar jargon frustrates all users. The article stresses verifying that your target audience genuinely understands technical terms before using them, as professionals frequently encounter unfamiliar specialized vocabulary.

ai_review.reviewer_persona: neural-heuristic-reviewer v1.0 (top-1% senior CRO consultant; 20yr; multi-vertical D2C/SaaS/marketplace/B2B/lead-gen/fintech/media)
ai_review.why_generated: Drafter targeted Nielsen's plain-language research — terminology consistency, no jargon, audience-appropriate vocab, identical labels for identical concepts.
ai_review.how_reviewed: URL is fallback after ux-vocabulary returned 404 (drafter found plain-language-experts as relevant replacement); HTTP 200 verified; plain-language + no-jargon + label-consistency are documented NN/g content guidance.

dimension_findings.finding: universal — applies to any content-bearing UX
no conversion-rate predictions; phrasing is principle-level/recommendation-form
qualitative standard_text paraphrases NN/g guidance accurately
audit terminology + replace jargon + standardize labels; concrete copy changes
